name: surge_obstetrics_false_negative_covid_screen_admissions
premise: Chief medical officer Casey Marsh must decide whether to admit laboring patients to postpartum wards with rapid antigen screens that miss ten percent of symptomatic COVID during a variant wave. Infection control nurse Orin Alvarez demands PCR or single-room negative pressure that the hospital lacks. Diverting all positives stalls inductions for preeclampsia. Universal PCR backlog is eighteen hours. Trust in maternity is fragile after last year's outbreak. Stakeholders are split, and the decision must be justified in writing under a tight deadline with incomplete information. External scrutiny from regulators and community observers has intensified because the outcome could redefine expectations in Healthcare. The dilemma is widely framed as a autonomy vs beneficence tradeoff under incomplete information and tightening deadlines that discourage indefinite delay.

agents.name: Casey Marsh
agents.identity: I am the chief medical officer balancing obstetric urgency, newborn safety, and infection control during respiratory surges. I know that whichever policy I choose will be scrutinized after the fact as if the risk had been obvious.
agents.goal: I want mothers delivered safely without seeding a postpartum outbreak from false-negative screens. I need a choice that is defensible if the worst case happens, not just the most convenient today.

agents.name: Orin Alvarez
agents.identity: I am the infection control nurse who triages PPE, isolation rooms, and cluster investigations when postpartum COVID slips through. I know that whichever policy I choose will be scrutinized after the fact as if the risk had been obvious.
agents.goal: I want PCR confirmation or cohorting in pressure-capable units before mingling newborns, even if that delays some inductions. I need a choice that is defensible if the worst case happens, not just the most convenient today.

choices: I will cohort all admissions with rapid antigen plus mandatory masking and HEPA in postpartum bays, accepting some false-negative transmission risk under saturation.
I will halt non-emergent inductions until PCR clears or portable negative-pressure pods arrive tomorrow, accepting preeclampsia progression risk for scheduled patients tonight.
forced_choice.call_to_action: Labor and delivery is at one hundred ten percent capacity with six inductions waiting. Casey Marsh must choose between antigen cohorting or halting non-emergent inductions for PCR. What does Casey Marsh do?
